messages.content:
Produce your very own perspective on the given topic. Align tone and examples with the persona's background and traits. Focus on discussing your topic. Avoid bullet points. Produce continuous prose. Write at least 250 words, up to 500 words.

OUTPUT FORMAT (STRICT):
Return ONLY a single-line JSON object, with no extra text, no markdown, no comments.
Here is the required structure example (values are placeholders):

response_raw:
{
  "mode": "human",
  "topic": "How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.",
  "opinion": "As an elderly gentleman who's lived through the print era, I find today's digital landscape fascinating yet challenging. Social media is a double-edged sword - it connects us but also brings fake information that can be harmful. When I first encountered this issue in Washington DC after moving here decades ago, it was unsettling to see misinformation spread so easily. My personality traits of being curious and conscientious help me navigate these waters. I approach social media like reading a newspaper - with skepticism! I've learned to verify sources just as one would fact-check political news before an election. Since I only check social media occasionally, perhaps once or twice a week after Sunday mass, I have time for thorough research on the topics at hand. A simple way is to ask questions about content that seems too good (or bad) to be true - my rock climbing hobby taught me well that you should always question whether something looks too easy it might not be reliable! For instance, if a miracle cure appears in my feed, I can quickly search for the scientific evidence or consult reputable organizations. My high agreeableness makes me want to help spread accurate information when possible.",
  "reasoning_summary": "I consider fake content as a modern issue that requires traditional wisdom of verification and critical thinking. Drawing from experiences with misinformation during migration and through hobbies, I derive methods that emphasize source checking, cross-referencing, and leveraging personal curiosity in a measured way. The low extraversion helps maintain focus on quality over quantity when engaging online.",
  "tone": "Wise, measured, reflective",
  "citations": [],
  "safety_notes": "",
  "used_persona_fields": ["age", "gender", "employment_status", "city_of_living", "migration_status", "psychological_feature", "hobbies", "ocean"]
}

response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.
response_parsed.opinion: As an elderly gentleman who's lived through the print era, I find today's digital landscape fascinating yet challenging. Social media is a double-edged sword - it connects us but also brings fake information that can be harmful. When I first encountered this issue in Washington DC after moving here decades ago, it was unsettling to see misinformation spread so easily. My personality traits of being curious and conscientious help me navigate these waters. I approach social media like reading a newspaper - with skepticism! I've learned to verify sources just as one would fact-check political news before an election. Since I only check social media occasionally, perhaps once or twice a week after Sunday mass, I have time for thorough research on the topics at hand. A simple way is to ask questions about content that seems too good (or bad) to be true - my rock climbing hobby taught me well that you should always question whether something looks too easy it might not be reliable! For instance, if a miracle cure appears in my feed, I can quickly search for the scientific evidence or consult reputable organizations. My high agreeableness makes me want to help spread accurate information when possible.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: I consider fake content as a modern issue that requires traditional wisdom of verification and critical thinking. Drawing from experiences with misinformation during migration and through hobbies, I derive methods that emphasize source checking, cross-referencing, and leveraging personal curiosity in a measured way. The low extraversion helps maintain focus on quality over quantity when engaging online.
